videos.title: Mistakes American Tourist Make in England
videos.description:
Whether you are heading to Lisbon, Bournmouth, Bristol, Manchester or Newcastle there are a lot of do's and some really important don'ts of visiting England and we want to make sure you don't make any tourist mistakes when you visit England. So here we have the most common mistakes tourists make when they go to England on vacation. Honest travel advice for visiting England.
Filmed in York, Liverpool, and Chester, England, UK

videos.transcript: Hey, fellow travelers, Mark here with Tourist World. Today, we're here in Chester, England, and today we'll talk about are some of the mistakes that travelers make when they do come here to England because this is a beautiful country. There's so much to see and do outside of just going to London that a lot of people miss out on. And we all make mistakes when we travel. We want to make sure that you don't make mistakes when you do come here. And I think one mistake actually just comes with timing. One of the things I see the most troubles with the tourists have when they come here is the idea of half seven. If someone says half seven to an American, they're like, "What? Half of seven is three and a half." But the thing is, when you're telling time in England, half seven means 7:30, okay? Now, if you're in Germany or some other place, half seven actually means 6:30. [music] But here, half seven is 7:30. That's where I've seen a lot of miscommunication with tourists and travelers when it comes to making reservations for restaurants or train tickets. So, remember, half seven is 7:30, okay? Now, another mistake that tourists make when they go around England is they forget to take advantage of the deals cuz there's so many deals you can get, whether it's on train tickets, it's going out to eat, getting the extra specials. There's all kinds of stuff. [music] Heck, not using the National Trust when you're going around to see the stately manners of the the the homes around the country. There's so many places that put together deals where if you buy a pass for a few days, it will really pay for itself. So, really make sure you're doing your research on how you can save some money when you come here cuz honestly, I know every time we come here, we get our National Trust pass and it helps us get into a bunch of different stately homes and manor houses and all kinds of things. And that really helps save our budget and we get to see more when we're there cuz we're not worried about well, how much is the next place going to cost? How much is the next place going to cost? It all kind of goes together. So, make sure you look for those things. And kind of going off of the National Trust is also I see tourists make the mistake of not staying in historic inns and bed and breakfasts. Look, when you're going around, if you're in a city like this here in Chester, there's so many historic inns you can go stay in. Place I'm staying with has been an inn since the 1500s. Yes, the steps are a little wobbly and they're they're kind of wonky and stuff like that, but the building's like 500 years old. What do you expect? And that's one of the things that can really make this more of a cultural adventure when you're here and to really get a better vibe and better feel for everything [music] is staying at those historic inns, staying at those bed and breakfasts, and not just going to a standard hotel. Now, you can, there's nothing wrong with that, but if you're going to be going to these villages, you're going to need to look for and and towns, you're going to be to looking for local accommodation because there's not always a chain hotel in every single town you go to or city you want to go to in England. So, make sure you're a little bit more open-minded with your accommodation options. Now, another thing people say that keeps them from coming to England is they think it's it's going to be a very expensive trip and and yes, if you're going to go to London, London will be a very expensive trip. I will not deny that. That's going to be one of the most expensive cities you can go to as a traveler. But here's the thing, if you come up here to the north of England, you go other places in the south of England, or the Midlands, prices are significantly less than you go to London. And so, don't think that oh, England is going to be a super expensive vacation. No, it's not. I know for me, I've spent 2 weeks between York, Liverpool, and Chester, and I've spent the same amount of money as 1 week in London, okay? So, kind of think about that when you're looking at where you're going to go because choosing where you go in the country can have a big impact on your budget when you come here. Now, another thing I see tourists making a mistake about when they come here to England is not respecting the queue culture. [music] Look, line culture, queue culture, standing in line is very much a thing here. And in my video on how to upset the locals, you know, kind of piss them off when you're here, [music] that's like number one of the things that will upset people. And you have to realize is if you come to a place where everyone kind of bum rushes the door when the subway opens or the train opens, or whoever can just get in front of people gets to go first, that's not how it works here. People very much set up a nice line, nice queue, they wait in line, they don't complain about it, they wait there, you know, stiff upper lip, we're just going to wait until it's our turn. That's how it is here. And if you go against that, people are not going to be out with you. They're going to say something to you and and and sometimes you don't realize it cuz maybe you're from hey, whoever gets to the front is to the front. Doesn't matter if there's a line or not, but that is a mistake I see a lot of international travelers making here. Another mistake that tourists make is sometimes they don't realize the differences between England, Scotland, Wales, Britain, Great Britain, the United Kingdom, the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland. You know, you have all these different words you'll hear about talking about different places around here. I think it's really important you realize that if somebody's from England, they're English. If they're from Wales, they're Welsh. They're not English. If they're from Scotland, they're a Scot. They're not English, okay? And so that's one thing that tourists don't always know and they sometimes upset people because if you call an Englishman a Scot, there's a connotation to that. If you call a Scottish person English, there's a connotation to that as well. So, you do want to be careful with that cuz that's just a simple mistake that tourists do make. And another thing is when you're traveling around, a lot of people make the mistake of I'm only going to stay in cities and I'm only going to stay in towns when I come here to England. And I got to tell you, there's a lot of natural beauty when [music] you come here, whether you're going to the Yorkshire Dales or you're going to go to the Peak District or you're going to go to the Lake [music] District, which is just gorgeous. See the Cliffs of Dover. Going around, you have to realize there's tons of like uh hiking trails. There's tons of walking trails. There's all kinds of nature you can have when you're here when you come to England. It's not just going from city to city to city. It's not like you're doing a football tour, you know, around going London for Chelsea, Manchester for Manchester United, Liverpool for Everton. Nada. You can go around and enjoy the nature that's here as well, which a lot of people don't really think about because they think of England and industrialization and London, whereas honestly, there's a lot of beautiful nature when you're here, okay? So, let's hop to another part of England to uh talk about some more mistakes that tourists make. So, now we're here in York, outside of York Minster. And my next mistake that tourists make when they come to England is they think that it rains all the time every day when you're here in England. And that's not true. I've been here a number of times in the summer where I was was literally for 10 days and it didn't rain at all. Like, it was sunny, I got sunburn. I mean, you don't think about sunburn in England when you come here, but it does happen. So, don't think it rains all the time. Now, I know it's cloudy now, but just be aware that it's not a torrential downpour every single day when you're here in England, okay? Now, having said that, I I do want to say another mistake is not bringing rain gear when you come because yes, it probably will rain sometime when you're here. I won't lie to you. I remember I was here for New Year's, it was like raining sideways, and my socks were wet. It was crazy time. But, have yourself. Like, my backpack, I have a rain poncho in there in case it does rain. So, do be prepared for that. But, don't think it's going to rain all the time. But, just be prepared in case it does. Now, the next costly mistake I see tourists make when they come to England is they disrespect or disregard the coins. Look, the coins you get when you come here, they're actually worth something. It's not like in the US where even if you have like a lot of coins, it might be, you know, 82 cents, but you have a handful of coins. Here, if you have a handful of coins, you could probably pay for dinner cuz you got maybe a £2 coin or £1 coin. I mean, 50 cent piece. I mean, money coins really go farther here than in like in the US and other places. So, just be aware of that. And don't just leave them behind or throw them in the fountain. Use them to buy some cool souvenirs or buy yourself a snack or buy yourself dinner, which I've done a number of times with the coins I've had here. And when you are ordering, another mistake I see tourists make is um they order an ale and they expect a beer. Look, when you get an ale here in England, they actually pull it. It's like a pump. They're actually pulling it to get it out, all right? And you have those this ales when you're here, and they're very different than your you know, your Bud Light or your Budweiser or your Stella Artois, that's that bubbly cold ice cold beer that that tickles your nose. No, ales are very different than your traditional kind of beer you kind of think of when you're thinking of getting a beer with your buddies and crack off a top and do this. No, they're going to pull that beer, okay? So, when you order an ale, don't expect a beer, expect an ale. It is a different experience. And yes, that means it's not going to be ice cold. It's going to be close to room temperature than ice cold, but it's still going to be fantastic. Now, the next mistake I see tourists making is they think London is England. Yes, London is in England, and London has a lot of stuff that's in England, but don't [music] think just because you went to London, you saw England. There's a reason why I'm filming this in York and other places around England, so I can show you this country has so much more than just London. It's like saying I went to New York City and I saw the US. I went to Paris and I saw France. I went to Tokyo, I saw Japan. There's so much more throughout England you can go and enjoy. Here in the north when you're going to York, it's a great time. Or or maybe you're going to go over to the Liverpool and see the where the Beatles were. Or maybe you go down to Brighton or you go to the Cliffs of Dover. I mean, you go around England, there's so much more to do here than doing London. So please, even if you're only going to be going to London, do some of the day trips to like, you know, go down to Brighton, go to Canterbury, go to Bath, go see some of the other parts because don't think that it's just London you're here. That's a mistake a lot of people make. Go and explore this country cuz it is beautiful. And when you're going to explore this beautiful country, another mistake I see tourists make is they get scared off of driving because they drive on a different side of the road than you drive in back home, so they're scared to drive around. Look, it's okay to be scared driving in London cuz even the locals are scared to drive in London, but driving around England is wonderful. You can go and explore so much more, see more castles, see more stately homes, see, you know, the different national parks around here. There's so many great things to see and do that you need to have a car to truly enjoy. And so many tourists make the mistake of, "Yeah, I guess the other side of the road, I roundabouts scare me. I don't want to do this." No, don't make that mistake. You'll be okay. The first roundabout or two will scare you a little bit, but after that, you'll be fine. And you'll be surprised when you get away from London how little traffic you're going to have, so you can enjoy it and not freak out about all the driving and all the other side of the road stuff. And when people actually do start driving around England, they start to realize another mistake they made. And that is thinking that English food is just fish and chips and bangers and mash and other pub grub kind of stuff. Look, England has a lot of immigration from all over the world that's come here and they've brought their food with them. So, you could have fantastic food that is not just straight from a fry-up. You could have so much great food when you're here that's not just an English breakfast or something like that. You could have non-English food when you're here and have a lot of great food as well. And it's not just that bangers and mash, fish and chips, and hitting the pub for a grub. No, there's a lot more than the chippy when you do come here. And with that food, another mistake I see tourists make is when they go to a pub, they think people are going to come take their order. Look, if you're going to a pub and you see a number on your table, realize that probably no one's going to come and take your order. You need to go to the bar to put your order in and then you tell them what your table number is and then someone will bring it out to you, okay? Cuz I've been in a number of pubs I mean a number of pubs in England over the years where tourists are just sitting there going, "Well, no one's come to serve us. What's going on here?" Look, don't make that mistake. Just go up, order at the bar, and you'll be fine. Just take your number up so you can pay and they know where to bring it, okay? Now, if you're going to be traveling by train around England, which is super easy to do, another thing I have to say, another mistake I see people make is they don't get a rail card. Look, if you get a rail card, you can get some serious discounts on train purchases because the trains in England and going around Great Britain overall, they can be a little pricey and saving some money just by having a rail card can go a long way. So, if you're going to be coming here for like 10 days, you're going from London to York to Liverpool to Chester, you know, to Wales to up to Scotland wherever, those rail cards can be a big discount for you and help you save a lot of money. So, don't be afraid to buy one even if you're only here for a few days because honestly, it can really save you some really good money and pay for itself. So, another mistake tourists make when they come here is really trying to understand the kind of love I'm not going to say love-hate. I'm going to say love/loathe relationship that England and English fans have with the England football team, the three lions. I mean, you'll be surprised how much if you listen to the radio, how much they love England, how England really should win every World Cup and every European Championship and every Champions League and every UEFA Cup, everything ever. England should win every single soccer football tournament ever ever ever ever. And you'll hear them like just they'll It's It's crazy how much like we are the best. It's coming home. It's coming home. Like you have this crazy mentality have for the English team. But then on the same thing, if things don't go well, if they miss a penalty kick or a kick stop or or they sky it over the bar, how people really turn on the team like oh, the manager needs to go. We need someone else here. This team is horrible. Like it's just crazy how much they can be so happy and so yes, England. But then at the same time go oh, I hate them. I mean, it's It's one of those things like tourists can't really understand and don't make the mistake of trying to understand because you can't. Like they have a whole other mentality when it comes to football than you have in any other country. So that love and loathing they have for the three lions, yeah, just just just don't be surprised. Don't make the mistake that you think you can understand it because you can't. And that's okay. So let's go to a different place to continue on our mistakes that tourists make when they come here to England. Now we're here in Liverpool and I think the next mistake I want to talk about is when people come to England they think when it says an 85% chance of rain that it's going to be a complete downpour the entire day. Look, the rain here is just kind of like rain. It just kind of comes and goes during the day. It's usually not a really heavy downpour. Yes, it does happen sometimes, but it's more of just like a kind of constant on and off rain. And And on days when it says it's going to be sunny, you might have a splash of rain pop in or on a day like today where it's supposed to be, you know, [music] raining all day long. Yes, there's some dark clouds over there, but across the way it's all sunny. So don't make the mistake of thinking that the weather's going to be one way or not or that downpours are going to come if they say it's going to rain. It's more of a a light constant drizzle most of the time, all right? Now since I'm here in Liverpool, I think it's important to mention this mistake, wearing a Manchester United jersey here in Liverpool. Well, not just that, but I think a lot of tourists think that, "Oh, I'm going to go to England. I'll get my new t-shirt, my new jersey for my favorite team, whether it's Manchester, Liverpool, Chelsea, whatever, and then they're just going to wear it out when they go to the pub, when they go around and see the sights." And And I think that's a mistake that tourists make because most of the time it doesn't matter. People don't really care. But, if it's like a game day, or people are getting a little bit too exuberant, or the lads have had a few much too many drinks, that can be something you want to think about. So, get your jersey when you're here. If you're going to go to the game, go ahead and wear it. But, if you're not, save it until you get home. Now, another mistake I see tourists make when they come to England is they have the wrong adapter. When you come here, you want an adapter like this. See how it's these three flat things here? That's what you want to have. That's what you're looking for for the UK in general, okay? So, if you're looking to get an adapter, I recommend you get like a universal adapter that'll have like Europe, and it'll have the US, and then it'll have the UK. So, make sure you have the right adapter. Now, a mistake that even seasoned travelers sometimes make when they come to England is, if you go to Europe, or you or you know, mainland Europe, you're in Germany, or Spain, or or you're in the US even, or in Canada, you're used to having just one company run all the train lines. And that's a mistake you make when you come here to England because there's literally, it feels like dozens of different companies that actually run the train lines depending where you're going to go throughout the country. And so, when you go online, you go to the National Rail website, and you you go to book your ticket, and then it kicks you off to another website, people are like, "Oh, is this a scam?" No, they're going to send you to the website of the company that runs the train line that you want to get. So, for example, I'm here for a 12-day trip. I've had to sign up for four different programs on four different train lines to get myself around the country. So, don't think there's just one train line when you're here. And another mistake, and it doesn't matter if you're at the the railway station, or you're at the bus station, or you're at a museum, or you're walking around the city, is that's when tourists don't ask for assistance when they're here in England because they have a great system here to help out tourists and help out people, but also the people here are pretty friendly and they will help you out. I mean, and it doesn't matter if you're lost in London or lost in Leeds, someone will help you if you ask. And the thing is, the police are very helpful here, museum guides are helpful here, your concierges are here, or just a dude walking down the street with a purse you're sitting by on the train, you can talk to them. I mean, here in Liverpool, the people are more than outgoing and give you advice if you look lost. They'll be like, "Hey mate, you lost?" You know, so so don't be afraid to ask for help when you're here. And you're going to maybe want to ask that help, especially if you want to go shopping late, cuz I think that's a that's a mistake that tourists make when they come to England. They think, "Oh, it's going to be open late night. I can go shopping in the evening. It'll be great." No. I mean, that is a big mistake you make when you come here. You need to realize, if you're in London, yeah, the shops stay open longer, right? And the museums might stay open longer. But, if you're here in the north, you're in the Midlands or or wherever you're going to be going around, you have to realize that some places the museums might close at 4:00. The stores might close at 5:00 or 6:00 p.m. I mean, that's one of the things you have to kind of think about when you're here is, "Hey, when do I need to get everything done?" So, make sure you're looking for the schedules of all the places you want to visit, so you can make sure you can get in, cuz you don't want to miss out on going to some of the great museums that are here or the historic homes, all right?

Embark on a captivating journey to the vibrant city of Bristol, UK, as we unveil the top things to do and see in this cultural hub. This immersive video will take you on a virtual tour of Bristol's rich history, stunning architecture, and lively atmosphere.

Explore Bristol's historic harbor, a bustling hub of activity and a testament to the city's maritime heritage. Visit the iconic SS Great Britain, a beautifully restored Victorian ship that offers a fascinating glimpse into the city's seafaring past. Take a leisurely stroll along the waterfront, soak in the vibrant atmosphere, and enjoy panoramic views of the harbor.

Discover Bristol's thriving street art scene, with works by renowned artist Banksy and other talented local artists adorning the city's walls. Explore the colorful streets of Stokes Croft and Bedminster, and admire the vibrant murals and thought-provoking pieces that make Bristol a haven for street art enthusiasts.

Indulge in Bristol's vibrant food and drink scene, with an array of restaurants, cafes, and markets to tantalize your taste buds. Sample delicious local delicacies such as the famous Bristol cider, artisanal cheeses, and the iconic Bristol blue glass.

Immerse yourself in the city's rich cultural offerings. Visit the Bristol Museum and Art Gallery to explore its diverse collection of art and historical artifacts, or enjoy a performance at the Bristol Old Vic, the oldest continuously working theater in the English-speaking world.

Experience the beauty of nature in Bristol's lush parks and green spaces. Take a peaceful walk through the picturesque Brandon Hill Park, visit the stunning gardens of the University of Bristol Botanic Garden, or explore the vast Blaise Castle Estate with its ancient woodlands and historic mansion.

Join us as we uncover the hidden gems and must-visit attractions of Bristol, a city known for its creativity, innovation, and vibrant energy. Let us transport you to a world of captivating beauty, cultural richness, and unforgettable experiences.

Get ready to be captivated by the allure of Bristol in this immersive video experience. So, pack your bags, and let the adventure begin in this remarkable city where history, art, and creativity converge!

videos.description:
Planning a trip to Bristol, UK in 2025? 🇬🇧 Then look no further! This comprehensive Bristol travel guide showcases the top 10 things to see and do in and around this vibrant city. From iconic landmarks and historic sites to beautiful green spaces and exciting day trips, I'll cover the best of Bristol and its stunning surroundings.

In this video, you'll discover:
0:21 - Clifton Suspension Bridge
Marvel at Isambard Kingdom Brunel's engineering masterpiece and enjoy breathtaking views from Clifton Downs.
1:15 - Bristol Harbour
Explore the historic Harbourside, Wapping Wharf, and see the iconic cranes that tell tales of Bristol's maritime past.
2:28 - Canon's Marsh & Millennium Square
Experience modern Bristol with its vibrant atmosphere and attractions.
2:42 - Bristol Cathedral
Step inside this magnificent cathedral on College Green, a haven of peace and stunning architecture.
3:23 - Park Street & Banksy Artwork
Wander up one of Bristol's most famous streets and spot some original Banksy street art.
3:57 - Bristol Museum & Art Gallery
Delve into art, history, and culture at this fascinating museum.
4:34 - Brandon Hill & Cabot Tower
Climb Cabot Tower for panoramic views over Bristol.
5:10 - SS Great Britain
Step aboard another of Brunel's masterpieces, the historic SS Great Britain, for a journey back in time.

Bonus Day Trips & Nearby Attractions:
5:59 - The Cotswolds (Dyrham Park)
Experience the quintessential English countryside with a visit to Dyrham Park in the beautiful Cotswolds.
6:30 - Lacock
Explore the picturesque village of Lacock, a National Trust gem often used as a film location (Harry Potter, Downton Abbey).
6:54 - Cheddar Gorge
Witness the stunning natural beauty of Britain's largest gorge.
7:13 - Bath
Discover the UNESCO World Heritage city of Bath, famous for its Roman Baths and Georgian architecture.

videos.transcript: Hello everyone and welcome to the vibrant and historic city of Bristol. So in this video, I am excited to share my top 10 things to do in Bristol. So, we begin our adventure in the expansive green hills that overlook the city because here we can find the iconic Clifton Suspension Bridge. Could you imagine that uh just a short 20-minute walk from the literal center of Bristol and you get here? This bridge spans the dramatic Avon Gorge and dates all the way back to 1864. It was designed by the renowned architect Ismbbard Kingdom Brunell, which you will hear much more about towards the end of the video. But the views from this spot just beside Clifton Observatory are absolutely breathtaking. And what's more, from here you can enjoy so many walks through the varied nature of the Clifton Downs as Bristol is just a city surrounded with peaceful natural green scenery. But we're actually heading away from all that down the hill towards the heart of the city, which I would say is Bristol Harbor. This bustling area is filled with restaurants and bars. Some of the most interesting are housed inside these shipping containers at the trendy Whopping Warf, making it a perfect place to get a bite to eat. The harbor, though, is also home to a long and interesting history. What you see today was mostly designed in the early 1800s with Bristol being a major port. And many of these original elements are still on show today, like these historic cranes for example, the the old train tracks here and this specific crane, which is actually the only one of its kind in the entire world. And it's something that you would just so easily walk past. And Bristol is full of things like this to keep an eye out for whilst exploring. This harbor really was the point where we began to see just how unique Bristol is. And I think it's the perfect place to go to immediately feel the effect that this city can have on you. From here, let's walk slightly inland through Canon's Marsh, which is a lively area right beside the harbor. There are so many museums and fun things to do in this creative part of the city. So, I would definitely suggest a nice stroll through it, especially to get to our next stop, which is Bristol Cathedral. This magnificent building dates back to the 12th century, and it's a stunning example of Gothic architecture. We were blown away by the intricate details and the peaceful atmosphere inside. But this cathedral is even more than that to be honest because it's located inside this beautiful green space. A great place to relax on a sunny day, I'm sure with it being surrounded by these grand buildings before continuing your adventure through Bristol. And to do that, we don't actually have to walk far because the square is located right at the foot of Bristol's most famous shopping street. We did some fun window shopping here. We actually popped into a small bookstore. Just picked up a cool book from the £5 or less bookshop and they had some really good titles in there. So, and of course stopped off to admire Banksy's well-hunged lover artwork which if you look very carefully you can spot just hidden here somewhere upon this street. This street is a must visit, not just for its shops and beautiful view from the top, but what you can find at the end of it, which is the Bristol Museum and Art Gallery, which is honestly one of my favorite museums in England because it really has something for everyone, from Egyptian mummies to dinosaur skeletons and a fine art gallery right at the top. It's the perfect size, giving you just enough to spend a couple of hours around here and really give you a bit of a snapshot of so much history and art on display. Now, as I mentioned back at the start of the video, Bristol is a very green city, and the parks here are another real highlight, I think. For example, after the museum, we headed to Brandon Hill Park, which is actually the oldest public park in the city. And it's a perfectly peaceful place offering panoramic views of the city, and also the Cabo Tower, which you can see sticking up from the center of the park. You can even climb up it if you like to get even more stunning views. And also earlier in the video, you may remember I mentioned Ismbbard Kingdom Brunell. And he didn't only design that suspension bridge. He also designed three iconic ships. One of which is right here in Bristol. This is the SS Great Britain, often called the world's first Great Ocean Liner. Today you can explore this entire ship from the decks to the engine room. And you can even go down into the dry dock to see some of the ship's hull from below. Which fun fact, although this ship made countless voyages across the Atlantic, the dry dock that it sits in today was actually the very dry dock that it was built in all those years ago. Now, I did also promise I would tell you a little bit about Bristol's unique surroundings as well as the city. So, let me just give you a few ideas now for a great day trip from Bristol. Obviously, you can venture into the picturesque Cotswwells that sit just north of Bristol. One beautiful place just a 20-minute drive from Bristol is Durham Park with its stunning gardens and deer park. [Music] And slightly further a field if you want that sort of Cotswald village vibe is Lakeok. And this one is not just a charming village because it's actually famous for being used as a set for Hogwarts in the Harry Potter films. Both these places are perfect to escape from the hustle and bustle of the city for a little bit. Another incredible day trip from Bristol and going south this time is actually Cheddar Gorge. Famous throughout the entirety of England, this dramatic limestone natural wonder with towering cliffs is just a stunning place to explore. And finally, just a 30inut drive from Bristol, you can find the unmissable historic city of Bath. Bath is famous for its Roman baths, which are some of the best preserved Roman architecture in Britain. It's also famous for its iconic Georgian architecture and its beautiful natural surroundings, really making it a perfect day trip from Bristol. And if you do like the look of this city, by the way, please do feel free to check out my full top 10 things to do in Bath for an even more in-depth look into this beautiful city. And so, there you have it. Those are my top 10 things to do in and around Bristol. We absolutely loved exploring this vibrant city and its surrounding areas. There's so much to see and do. You could easily spend days here. Everything in this video was recorded within one day, so you can definitely do it. But I think I would certainly advise spend a little bit more time here to really discover the hidden places around every corner and see everything that Bristol has to offer. Let me know in the comments below if there's any other places that you think I missed out of this video. That way, we can all share ideas and explore even more of this beautiful city. videos.transcript: Think you know Bristol, think again. Today I'm taking you beyond the usual tourist spots to explore five unusual things to do in my incredible hometown Number one. I'm standing in the mirror maze at the University of Bristol because nothing says serious academic institution like a maze. This art installation is called follow me and the 76 vertical stainless steel plates are found in Royal Fort Garden a grade one listed Garden which is also worth a visit. Although a modern structure, it was erected in 2009 to mark the centenary of the University receiving its Charter While it's part of the University, it is free to visit and is open to the public It's pretty, it warps its surroundings and let's face it, it's a great spot for a selfie Number 2 and just around the corner is Palm Temple. This work of art is by Luke Jerram. You know the guy responsible for the inflatable Moon and Earth popping up across the city. It is located outside the University of Bristol's chemistry Department and like the mirror maze it's free and open to the public. Inside is a bell which tolls between 150 to 200 times a day at random intervals it's called the extinction bell and each toll represents the loss of a species across the planet a beautiful spot for much darker, more complex contemplation, typical Bristol Number 3 The twisted sculptures by Alex Chinneck. If you want a cheesy photo come to Cheese Lane You know that feeling when you think reality is broken Chinneck's work bends, warps and twists traditional British objects that would have once been the cornerstone of Great Britain streets. It's like a glitch in The Matrix while you're there check out Jimmy Hendrix by David Blatch on the side of the Bridge Inn Number 4 Bristol's own Leaning Tower, Move over Pisa, Temple Church recently reopened to the public and is yet to be explored by many Bristolians. The Leaning Tower and walls of this large medieval Church survived bombing during the second World War. While most of the church was lost during the war, it wasn't the bomb damage that caused the tower to lean. Like the tower in Pisa, it was unintentionally built that way due to subsidence If you like the T-shirt I am wearing in this scene you can grab your own from TheBristolshop.co.uk It's my Online Marketplace dedicated to supporting local artists where you can shop all things Bristol. Gert Lush Temple Church is so called because the original church here was built by the Knights Templar Their round church was later replaced with a more spacious rectangular one but the shape of the original church is marked out in the ground which is pretty cool okay this isn't technically in my top five but while you're here it's a great idea to wet your whistle in the King's Head, It's a grade two listed Pub built in the 17th century and now serves some of the city's finest local ales, ciders and spirits If you can, enjoy a drink in the Tram Car Bar, a snug that was added in the late 19th or early 20th century. Number five. The Giant's cave. Moving on to one of Bristol's quirkiest legends, the Giant's cave at Clifton Observatory. You know the Avon Gorge but have you stood halfway down the gorge inside a cave? This respectable looking Tower is Clifton Observatory and while you can visit for incredible views of the suspension bridge and a rare example of a camera obscura, this remarkable structure sits on something much more mysterious. For just a couple of pounds you can descend into the depths of the gorge through a man-made narrow tunnel and suddenly, bam, you're standing on a ledge halfway down the gorge The view is incredible and the cave itself has this mysterious edge that feels straight out of a fantasy novel the Giants cave at Clifton Observatory is part history, part legend and all kinds of weird This spot's tied to the legend of Goram and Ghyston, two giants who supposedly shaped The Gorge itself And there you have it five, well six of the weirdest and most wonderful things to do right here in Bristol.
